paragraphs: I am an astronomer based at the University of Warwick. I study some of the most extreme objects in the Universe: neutron stars, black holes, and white dwarfs that are actively stealing material from a companion star. My main interest is to understand how these extreme systems exchange mass and energy with their environment through outflows (disc winds and jets), and how this feedback governs their observable phenomenology and long-term evolution.

My work combines multi-wavelength observational astronomy with theoretical modeling to explore some of the most extreme environments in the universe. To catch these fast-changing events, I coordinate observations using many of the world's most powerful telescopes simultaneously — from the Hubble Space Telescope and the James Webb Space Telescope in orbit, to X-ray satellites and giant ground-based observatories in Chile and the Canary Islands. I have been awarded over 200 hours of observing time on these facilities as lead scientist, working with an international network of collaborators across four continents.

I am originally from Spain, I studied my Bachelor and Master at the University of La Laguna in the Canary Islands and completed my PhD at the University of Southampton before moving to Warwick. When I'm not observing, I help develop the instruments and software tools that future astronomers will rely on.

I actively mentor PhD and masters students, sit on telescope time allocation panels for international observatories, and contribute to open-source software projects that make astronomical data analysis more accessible and reproducible.
